coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive humidity sensing properties of carbon quantum dots films
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

Ming, Hai
Liu, Ruihua
Han, Xiao
Kang, Zhenhui
Liu, Yang
Zhang, Yonglai
coredata.dc:description: We reported the fabrication of a humidity sensing device based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) film. The conductivity of the CQDs film has a linear and rapid response to relative humidity, providing the opportunity for the fabrication of humidity sensing devices. The mechanism of our humidity sensor was proposed to be the formation of hydrogen bonds between carbon quantum dots and water molecules in the humidity environment, which significantly promote the electrons migration. In a control experiment, this hypothesis was confirmed by comparing the humidity sensitivity of candle soot (i.e. carbon nanoparticles) with and without oxygen containing groups on the surfaces.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Thin films
D. Electrical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots films
Preparation of functionalized carbon nanoparticles from candle soot

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM images of the synthesized CQDs. (b) Wide angle XRD pattern of the solid state CQDs. (c) The FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs. (d) Raman spectrum of the CQDs.

(a) The conductivity of the CQDs film as a function of the ambient relative humidity. (b) The response and recovery curve of the CQDs based humidity sensing device. The curves were measured between 7% and 43% RH at room temperature.
The schematic diagrams of the electrons’ migration routes.

(a) FT-IR spectra of the fresh and oxygen functionalized candle soot (carbon nanoparticles). (b) The conductivity as a function of the ambient relative humidity of the carbon films made from fresh (pink line) and oxygen functionalized (purple line) candle soot.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive humidity sensing properties of carbon quantum dots films

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM) and Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Super-Diamond and Advanced Films (COSDAF), Department of Physics and Materials Science, City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM) and Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou, China. Tel.: +86 512 65880957.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ► A humidity sensing device was fabricated based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) films. ► The conductivity of the CQDs films shows a linear and rapid response to atmosphere humidity. ► The humidity sensing property was due to the hydrogen bonds between the functional groups on CQDs.

We reported the fabrication of a humidity sensing device based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) film. The conductivity of the CQDs film has a linear and rapid response to relative humidity, providing the opportunity for the fabrication of humidity sensing devices. The mechanism of our humidity sensor was proposed to be the formation of hydrogen bonds between carbon quantum dots and water molecules in the humidity environment, which significantly promote the electrons migration. In a control experiment, this hypothesis was confirmed by comparing the humidity sensitivity of candle soot (i.e. carbon nanoparticles) with and without oxygen containing groups on the surfaces.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent years, carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs) including carbon nanoparticles, carbon nanofibers, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), fulleren, and especially graphene, have revealed a cornucopia of applications in various scientific fields such as catalysis, sensing, bioimaging, tissue engineering, optoelectronic and electronic devices due to their unique electronic, mechanical, optical and chemical properties [1–9]. As a member of CNMs, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) which are generally defined as small carbon nanoparticles with passivated surface states have aroused enormous research interests in both fundamental properties and practical applications. For example, CQDs possess strong photoluminescence in both visible and near infrared region. As compared with semiconductor quantum dots, which generally contain heavy metal such as cadmium and lead, CQDs exhibit low-toxicity and high biocompatibility, which make them excellent candidates in biochemical applications, for instance bioimaging [10–13]. Besides, CQDs are not only excellent electron donors but also good electron acceptors in photoexcited states [14]. These unique photoelectronic properties endow CQDs with great potential in photovoltaic areas [15–17].

To date, owning to their promising properties, CQDs have been prepared by various approaches, and they have been used in a wide range of applications including photocatalysis [15], solar cell [16–19], fluorescent sensor [20], and bioimaging, represented by a large number of literatures. However, as far as we know, the property of gas sensitivity over CQDs has not been reported yet. In fact, the unique properties such as abundant oxygen-contained functional groups on the surface, semiconductivity and the large surface areas make the CQDs a promising sensing material, especially for gas detection. Here we reported that the conductivity of CQDs film has a linear and rapid response (with high on-off ratio about 1×104) to the relative humidity of the ambient atmosphere. This kind of highly sensitive humidity sensing properties may be due to the adsorption of water molecules and the formation of hydrogen bonds among CQDs through the surface oxygen-contained groups, which acts as the electron migration routes between CQDs and water molecules.

The carbon quantum dots were synthesized through a method of electrochemical ablation of graphite [21]. In the synthetic process, two same graphite rods (99.99%, Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd., 13cm in length and 0.6cm in diameter) were vertically inserted in the electrolyte with 3cm above the liquid surface and used as both cathode and anode in a separation distance of 7.5cm. The electrolyte is ultrapure water (18.4MΩcm−1, 600mL) without any other additives. A static potential of 50V was imposed on the two electrodes by a direct current (DC) power supply for 10 days with intensely stirring. Subsequently, we obtained the CQDs solution by high-speed centrifuging (22,000rpm) to wipe off the large graphite particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots films
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of the CQDs in aqueous solution was determined to be about 1.2mg/mL. The CQDs films were fabricated by dropping 2mL CQDs aqueous solution on a piece of glass sheet (1cm×2cm) and dried in evacuated container at 25°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of functionalized carbon nanoparticles from candle soot
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, 500mg candle soot was mixed with 100mL 5M HNO3 and refluxed for 24h at 100°C. After cooled down to room temperature, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles were collected by centrifugation (10,000rpm for 10min). Alternatively, the mixture was first neutralized by Na2CO3, and then extensively dialyzed against deionized water through dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained with an FEI/Philips Tecnai 12 BioTWIN transmission electron microscope and a CM200 FEG transmission electron micro-scope, respectively. The normal TEM samples were prepared by dropping the solution onto a copper grid with polyvinyl formal support film and dried in air. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained with a Nicolet 360 spectrometer. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectrum was acquired by using an X’Pert-ProMPD (Holand) D/max-γAX-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.15406nm). Raman spectrum was collected on an HR 800 Raman spectroscope (J Y, France) equipped with a synapse CCD detector and a confocal Olympus microscope. The humidity sensor was fabricated based on the carbon quantum dots film with two copper foils as contact electrodes.

The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1(a), the obtained CQDs show uniform sizes distribution from 4nm to 8nm. High-resolution TEM images (inset of Fig. 1(a)) reveal that the CQDs have graphitic crystalline structure, which originates from the graphite rod used for the synthesis. In addition to the morphology of the CQDs, the solid state CQDs was also characterized by wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 1(b)), and the pattern shows very broad diffraction peaks at 25.5°, 42.5° and 44.5°, respectively, indicating the graphitic character of our CQDs samples. The broad peak at 25.5° could be attributed to the (002) peak of the graphitic materials, whereas the small peaks at 42.5° and 44.5° corresponds to (100) and (101) peak, respectively. The obvious broadening of the diffraction peaks is due to the small size of these particles, noting that the average size of CQDs is only ∼5nm.

The corresponding Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum in Fig. 1(c) shows that there are typical peaks around 3345cm−1 and 1401cm−1 which could be ascribed to the stretching vibrations and in-plane bending vibrations of OH, respectively. The peaks located around 1636cm−1 and 1720cm−1 indicate the existence of carbonyl (CO) and the peaks sat around 1254cm−1 and 643cm−1 could be ascribed to the in-plane stretching vibrations of CH. These results indicate that the CQDs are covered with oxygen functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl. Raman spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 1(d)) exhibited two sharp peaks located around 1361.3cm−1 and 1593.9cm−1, corresponding to D-band and G-band, respectively. The G-band is due to the in-plane bond vibrations of graphite lattice, whereas the D-band refers to the disorder band resulted by the vibrations of the graphite edges. The I D/I G ratio of the CQDs was calculated to be ∼1.2, which is almost higher than that of graphene oxide. The relative high D-band intensity could be explained as the small particle size and the presence of abundant oxygen containing groups on their surface.

The obtained CQDs film is about 3μm in thickness with a dense structure according to the scanning electron microscopy images of a representative sample (supporting information, Fig. S1). The inset of Fig. 2(a) shows the schematic illustration of the humidity sensing device based on the CQDs film. The controlled relative humidity environment was acquired by using saturated aqueous solutions of LiBr, CH3COOK, MgCl2, K2CO3, Mg(NO3)2, NaBr, NaCl, KCl and KNO3 in a closed glass container at a constant temperature of 25°C, yielding a relative humidity (RH) of 7%, 23%, 33%, 43%, 54%, 59%, 75%, 85% and 95%, respectively [22]. Interestingly, we found that the conductivity of the CQDs films shows a linear dependence on the humidity of the ambient atmosphere. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the conductivity of the CQDs film in a dry environment (relative humidity bellow 7%) is close to zero, whereas when the CQDs film is placed in an environment with relative humidity of 95%, the conductivity increases sharply to ∼35Ω−1 m−1, indicating the high-sensitivity to the environment humidity. In addition to the humidity/conductivity dependence, we also evaluated the response and recovery time of the CQDs based humidity sensor. Shown in Fig. 2(b) is the response/recover curve for 6 cycles. The curves were measured between 7% and 43% RH at room temperature. Notably, the response time was measured to be ∼25s and ∼60s for humidity-on and humidity-off processes, respectively. Beside the fast response time, the CQDs based humidity sensing device also shows excellent stability, even for a long-term test, both response time and response degree were almost consistent. In addition, the on-off ratio of the selected CQDs based humidity sensing device was estimated to be 1×104 which shows an excellent device properties (supporting information, Fig. S2).

To further clarify the humidity response in conductivity of our CQDs film, a sensing mechanism was proposed (Fig. 3 ). As the whole preparation procedure of CQDs was carried out in ultrapure water without any other chemical reagents, the CQDs aqueous solutions do not have any impurity residues, which significantly simplified the purification procedure. What is more, the pure CQDs aqueous solution is the most convincing to explore relevant properties of the CQDs itself. As discussed previously, the as prepared CQDs possess abundant oxygen-contained groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface, which is confirmed by FT-IR (Fig. 1(c)). When the humidity of the ambient environment is very low, the electrons migration between neighboring CQDs mainly depends on the jumping mode. Only those electrons with extreme high energy can accomplish the leap-migration process according the Boltzmann's law. As a result, the conductivity of the solid CQDs films in dry environment is very low. However, when the CQDs film was placed in the environment with high relative humidity, the oxygen-contained groups on the surface of the neighboring CQDs can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules, which would significantly promote the electrons migration, and therefore increase the conductivity obviously. The phenomenon had also been reported in the humidity sensor based on graphene oxide [22]. Considering the fact that electron migration between adjacent CQDs through the hydrogen bonds is much easier than that through the jumping mode, the conductivity of the solid CQDs film increases with the humidity of the ambient environment sensitively. In addition, based on the in situ IR spectra (supporting information, Fig. S3), we found that there is a small red shift about ∼20cm−1 of the OH and CO absorption peaks of the carbon quantum dots after being exposed to a humid environment from thoroughly dried state. This may be a proof of the formation of hydrogen bonds [23].

In order to prove the above mentioned hypothesis, a series of control experiment was carried out. In our experiments, candle soot which is composed of carbon nanoparticles with sizes ranging from tens to hundreds of nanometers was adopted for the fabrication of a similar humidity sensing device. Typically, the fresh candle soot film was superhydrophobic because of the absence of hydrophilic oxygen containing groups; this was confirmed by FT-IR spectrum shown in Fig. 4(a). In the test of the humidity/conductivity dependence, the conductivity of the fresh candle soot film was almost consistent with the increase of relative humidity from 7% to 95% (Fig. 4(b)). As a comparison, candle soot nanoparticles functionalized with oxygen-containing groups were prepared by refluxing the fresh candle soot in nitric acid at 100°C for 24h [24]. The presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups after acid treatment is confirmed by FT-IR spectrum shown in Fig. 4(a). Notably, by using this oxygen functionalized candle soot as sensing materials, the device shows the sensitivity to ambient humidity despite the variation is not as large as CQDs (Fig. 4(b)). In the further control experiments, we also investigated the humidity sensitivity of the graphite powder film (supporting information, Fig. S4). As expected, the result (supporting information, Fig. S5) shows that no humidity sensitivity was observed in graphite structures powder based sensing device. These results indicate that the oxygen functional groups on the surface of carbon nanoparticles were essential for the humid sensitivity, which therefore, confirms our previous hypothesis. On the other hand, the graphite powder is an excellent conductor that the electrons can facilely migrate through the continuous conduction band while the electrons migrate with much hindrance through the CQDs films by leaping among neighboring CQDs. Therefore, the small size (average 5nm) of CQDs lead to the very large surface area, which should be another important factor responsible for the humidity sensitive property of the sensing device. In addition, we also investigated the selective sensitivity of the CQDs film to other gases. The data showed that the conductivity of the CQDs films is sensitive to H2S and NH3 but not to N2 and H2 (supporting information, Fig. S6). These results are also a further demonstration of the humidity sensing mechanism. Only the gas molecule could form hydrogen bonds with two or more CQDs can they influence the conductivity of CQDs films.

In summary, a sensitive humidity sensing device based on CQDs prepared by electrochemical ablation of graphite electrodes was fabricated and investigated. The CQDs films were proved highly sensitive to the environmental humidity, represented by a linear dependence of the conductivity with the relative humidity. The oxygen containing groups on the surface was considered essential for the humidity sensing property of CQDs. Moreover, the high surface area and the excellent stability made the CQDs film a good candidate in the application for gas sensing. It is believable that, as a sister material of graphene, the CQDs would find broad applications in sensing devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (Nos. 2012CB825800 and 2013CB932702), National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Nos. 51132006, 21073127, 21071104, 61008014), a grant from the Research Grants Council of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, China (Project No. CityU102010), A Foundation for the Author of National Excellent Doctoral Dissertation of P R China (FANEDD, No. 200929), and A Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD). We also acknowledge the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 20110490156) and the Hong Kong Scholar Program (XJ2011014).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2012.11.056.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are supplementary data to this article:
